coaching.coaching: Pin down FieldSanitizer comment-fit method name and signature with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: FieldSanitizer comment-fit method name and signature

coaching.coaching: Plan how unenumerated InboundMapper fake edit targets will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: unenumerated InboundMapper fake edit targets

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Approach section with the remediation: update the Approach paragraph to explicitly state that adf.py's decode role goes to InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text (not FieldSanitizer), and that FieldSanitizer handles fit-to-limit only — making the single-source-of-truth explicit.
evidence: Approach: 'FieldSanitizer … gains the comment-fit surface … (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by … comment_limits.py AND … adf.py)'

Remediation item 1: '_normalize_comment_body calls adf.adf_to_text(body) to DECODE … It routes through the backend's InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text(body) … FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body) … serves the :324 compare-body truncation'

An implementer reading only the Approach section would assign the adf decode role to FieldSanitizer; the remediation section corrects this to InboundMapper — two different answers to the same design question.
findings.finding: The Approach section states FieldSanitizer gains 'fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py AND adapters/jira/adf.py', attributing adf.py's decode role to FieldSanitizer; but the Plan-review remediation section contradicts this by assigning the adf.py DECODE usage in outbound_comments.py:134 to InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text and reserving FieldSanitizer.fit_comment for pure fit-to-limit only.
findings.location: Approach section (FieldSanitizer paragraph) vs Plan-review remediation section (item 1)
scenarios: Implementer reads Approach, puts adf decode into FieldSanitizer, then remediation contradicts it — two conflicting implementations ship.
Reviewer checking AC2 ('behaves identically for Jira') cannot determine which surface to test the decode path against.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace 'fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py AND adapters/jira/adf.py' with 'fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py (the adf.py decode role is covered by InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text, not FieldSanitizer)'.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `outbound_comments.py:324` should call the existing `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment` (no Protocol change needed) or a new `fit_comment` method — and if a new method, add it explicitly to the Scope's `_backend.py` and `adapters/jira/backend.py` entries with a concrete signature.
evidence: Remediation text: 'FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body) (a pure fit-to-limit, delegating to comment_limits.truncate_comment_body, no send-side warning) serves the :324 compare-body truncation'
_backend.py line 141: `def sanitize_comment(self, body: str) -> str: ...` — the only comment-related method in the Protocol
`grep fit_comment` across entire repo returns zero matches
jira_fields.py _sanitize_comment (line 184) already delegates to truncate_comment_body, matching what the plan says fit_comment should do
_JiraSanitizer.sanitize_comment (adapters/jira/backend.py:62) already delegates to jira_fields._sanitize_comment
findings.finding: The plan's remediation section names `FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body)` as the method that will serve the `outbound_comments.py:324` compare-body truncation path, but no such method exists in the `FieldSanitizer` Protocol in `_backend.py` (which defines `sanitize_comment`), and `fit_comment` does not appear anywhere in the codebase. The Approach section says `FieldSanitizer` 'gains the comment-fit surface' without naming a method, and the existing `sanitize_comment` already delegates to `truncate_comment_body` (the same function `outbound_comments.py:324` currently calls). It is unresolved whether the plan intends to add a NEW `fit_comment` method to the Protocol, rename `sanitize_comment` to `fit_comment`, or simply reuse the existing `sanitize_comment`.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation section, item 1 ('Outbound-comment ADF DECODE'); Approach section ('FieldSanitizer … gains the comment-fit surface'); _backend.py FieldSanitizer Protocol
scenarios: An implementor reads 'fit_comment' in the remediation and adds a SECOND truncation method to the Protocol alongside sanitize_comment, creating a duplicate surface.
An implementor tries to rename sanitize_comment to fit_comment, breaking every existing call site (acli.py:72, acli_cli_ops.py:479) that already uses sanitize_comment.
The Scope section lists _backend.py for 'FieldSanitizer comment-fit surface' but without a concrete method name, the required Protocol change (if any) is underspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body)' in the remediation section with 'FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment(body)' (the existing Protocol method), since _JiraSanitizer.sanitize_comment already delegates to truncate_comment_body — matching exactly what the plan requires for the :324 path. No new Protocol method is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (and reflect in at least one AC) whether fit_comment is a new method added alongside the existing sanitize_comment, or a rename of it — use one name consistently throughout Approach, Scope, and Testing sections.
evidence: Approach: 'FieldSanitizer (already pinned in _backend.py … with sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str) gains the comment-fit surface'
Remediation item 1: 'FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body) (a pure fit-to-limit, delegating to comment_limits.truncate_comment_body, no send-side warning) serves the :324 compare-body truncation'
No acceptance criterion names either method, so the AC verify step cannot unambiguously reference the right entity.
findings.finding: The Approach section names the new FieldSanitizer method `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str` (describing it as 'already pinned'), while the Plan-review remediation section names a different method `fit_comment(body)` as the surface for the :324 compare-body truncation — leaving it unclear whether fit_comment is a new method alongside sanitize_comment, a rename, or the same method referenced by two names.
findings.location: Approach section (FieldSanitizer paragraph) vs Plan-review remediation section item 1
scenarios: Implementer adds fit_comment as a new method and leaves sanitize_comment unchanged — both exist with overlapping semantics.
Implementer treats them as the same method under different names and renames sanitize_comment to fit_comment — breaks existing callers of sanitize_comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a single table or bullet list (e.g. in Scope or Approach) that maps each outbound_comments.py call site (`:75-85`, `:88`, `:134`, `:324`) to its exact new method call (`InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` or `FieldSanitizer.fit_comment` or the existing `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment`), so an executor has an unambiguous wiring spec without needing to synthesize Approach + remediation.
evidence: Approach: 'FieldSanitizer...gains the comment-fit surface the outbound comment path actually needs (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py and adapters/jira/adf.py)'
Remediation item 1: '`FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body)` (a pure fit-to-limit, delegating to `comment_limits.truncate_comment_body`, no send-side warning) serves the `:324` compare-body truncation'
_backend.py scope line: 'FieldSanitizer comment-fit surface' — does not name the method or clarify whether it is in addition to `sanitize_comment`
Approach names `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str` as the existing surface but never says which new method name the outbound path will call at each call site
findings.finding: The plan uses two different method names for what appears to be the new FieldSanitizer comment-fit surface: 'fit_comment' in the remediation section ("`FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body)` serves the `:324` compare-body truncation") and implicitly 'sanitize_comment' in the Approach ("outbound_comments.py resolves the configured backend's sanitizer"). An executor reading only Approach may implement the outbound path calling `sanitize_comment` for all truncation, while the remediation intends `fit_comment` for compare-body and `sanitize_comment` (existing) for send-side — the plan never makes this dual-method split explicit in one place.
findings.location: Approach section (FieldSanitizer paragraph) vs. Plan-review remediation section (item 1, FieldSanitizer.fit_comment)
scenarios: An executor implements only `fit_comment` (the remediation name), leaving `sanitize_comment` untouched, then wires the `:75-85` formatting path to `fit_comment` — wrong semantics (no send-side warning dropped).
An executor implements only `sanitize_comment` calls throughout, never adding `fit_comment` — the `:324` compare-body path gets send-side behavior it shouldn't have.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a call-site mapping table under Approach or in a new 'Wiring spec' subsection:

| outbound_comments.py site | Old call | New call |
|---|---|---|
| `:68` / `_ADF_KEY` decode at `:134` | `adf.adf_to_text` | `inbound_mapper.normalize_rich_text(body)` |
| `:71` / `:88` fit-to-limit | `comment_limits.truncate_comment_body` | `sanitizer.fit_comment(body)` (new Protocol method) |

Also note that `sanitize_comment` (existing, send-side) is NOT used on these paths — eliminating the name collision.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `backend_support.py` (`_FakeInbound`) and `test_backend_neutrality.py` (`_RecordingInboundMapper`) to Scope as edit targets that must implement `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str` once the InboundMapper Protocol gains that method.
evidence: _backend.py InboundMapper Protocol currently has only map_remote_to_local; plan adds normalize_rich_text as a new Protocol method
_FakeInbound (backend_support.py:100-107) implements only map_remote_to_local
_RecordingInboundMapper (test_backend_neutrality.py:212-220) implements only map_remote_to_local
AC includes 'make typecheck green'; missing method on fakes = mypy/pyright error
Neither file appears in Scope or Plan-review remediation Scope additions
findings.finding: Two existing InboundMapper fakes — _FakeInbound in backend_support.py (line 100) and _RecordingInboundMapper in test_backend_neutrality.py (line 212) — are unenumerated edit targets: adding normalize_rich_text(body) to the InboundMapper Protocol requires them to implement it or typecheck (make typecheck) fails.
scenarios: `make typecheck` fails because _FakeInbound and _RecordingInboundMapper do not satisfy the updated InboundMapper Protocol structural contract
Any test that passes _FakeInbound or _RecordingInboundMapper where InboundMapper is type-annotated will produce a type error
findings.suggested_fix: Add `def normalize_rich_text(self, body): return body if isinstance(body, str) else str(body)` to both `_FakeInbound` and `_RecordingInboundMapper`, and enumerate both files in Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `fit_comment` is a new FieldSanitizer Protocol method (distinct from `sanitize_comment`) or whether `outbound_comments.py` should call the existing `sanitize_comment`; if new, add `backend_support.py` (`_FakeSanitizer`) to Scope.
evidence: Remediation item 1 says: 'FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body: str) -> str (a pure fit-to-limit, delegating to comment_limits.truncate_comment_body, no send-side warning)'
_backend.py:141 already defines sanitize_comment(self, body: str) -> str on FieldSanitizer Protocol
_JiraSanitizer.sanitize_comment already delegates to jira_fields._sanitize_comment which calls truncate_comment_body
_FakeSanitizer in backend_support.py:125 implements sanitize_comment but not fit_comment
Neither backend_support.py nor _JiraSanitizer appears in Scope
findings.finding: The plan's remediation section introduces 'FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body)' as if it is a new/distinct method on the Protocol, but the FieldSanitizer Protocol in _backend.py already has sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str and _JiraSanitizer in adapters/jira/backend.py already delegates it. If fit_comment IS a new separate method, _FakeSanitizer (backend_support.py:110-126) and _JiraSanitizer (adapters/jira/backend.py:50-63) are unenumerated edit targets; if it IS sanitize_comment under a new name, the plan is self-contradictory.
scenarios: If fit_comment is a new Protocol method: _FakeSanitizer and _JiraSanitizer both lack it, causing typecheck failures and FakeBackend's sanitizer to not satisfy the Protocol
If fit_comment is meant to be the existing sanitize_comment: the plan's naming inconsistency between sections will confuse implementation

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge `inbound_differ.py:210`'s `_load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit()` call as either in-scope (add to Scope and route through a port method, e.g. `OutboundMapper.fit_description` or extend `FieldSanitizer`) or explicitly out-of-scope with a rationale and follow-up ticket reference.
evidence: inbound_differ.py:44 imports _load_adf from inbound_fields (which loads 'rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.adf')
inbound_differ.py:210: local_val = _load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val) — a fit operation, not normalize_rich_text
The plan's approach section only routes ADF decode (dict→text) calls through normalize_rich_text; fit_text_to_adf_limit is a different operation with no named port equivalent
inbound_differ.py is not in Scope and not explicitly called out as an acknowledged out-of-scope remainder
findings.finding: inbound_differ.py at line 210 calls _load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val) directly via the ADF vendor module (imported from inbound_fields as _load_adf). This is a distinct ADF call site — not the normalize_rich_text surface — that remains a direct vendor-module invocation in a core differ after this ticket lands. The plan does not enumerate inbound_differ.py as an in-scope or explicitly out-of-scope call site.
scenarios: After this ticket, outbound_comments and inbound_translate are vendor-clean, but inbound_differ still calls _load_adf() from inbound_fields for fit_text_to_adf_limit — a Jira-specific vendor operation in a neutral core differ, which is the same category of leak this ticket targets
The sweep gate (checking for the literal string in package-root modules) will NOT catch this because the literal lives in inbound_fields, not inbound_differ — but the behavioral leak persists

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., 'revert the three deletions as a single atomic commit; the lazy-key path is restored because no structural changes are made to adf.py or comment_limits.py') to satisfy T4's reversibility requirement.
evidence: Remediation item 3: 'applier.py drops them from its inbound_translate import list and from __all__' — deletion with no stated rollback path.
Remediation item 2: 'Only the vendor internals _ADF_KEY_APPLIER … are deleted from inbound_translate.py' — deletion with no stated rollback path.
No section of the plan describes a feature-flag, expand-contract sequence, or git-revert procedure for restoring the deleted names if the new routing proves faulty in production.
findings.finding: The plan performs irreversible deletions (literal keys from outbound_comments.py and inbound_translate.py, three re-export names from applier.py's __all__) that are destructive steps, but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan — only acknowledgement that consumers are preserved. The T4 rubric requires a reversibility mechanism, not just acknowledgement.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation section item 2 and item 3 (applier.py scope addition); Acceptance Criteria (added AC, last bullet)
scenarios: New InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text routing silently produces wrong output in production; team wants to revert to the lazy-key path but there is no documented back-out procedure.
A consumer outside the scanned modules imports a deleted re-export from applier.py; without a rollback path the fix is ad-hoc.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: 'All three deletion changes (literal keys in outbound_comments.py/inbound_translate.py, re-exports in applier.py) are concentrated in a single PR. Rolling back is a git revert of that PR; no data or schema changes are involved, so the revert is safe at any point after merge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AGENTS.md / ADR entry (or inline docstrings in `_backend.py` captured in a doc-update scope item) recording the semantics and rationale for the two new Protocol members (`FieldSanitizer.fit_comment` and `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text`) so future adapter authors have a machine-findable contract reference.
evidence: Plan states 'FieldSanitizer … gains the comment-fit surface' and 'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str' but lists no documentation artifact in Scope or AC.
No ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update appears anywhere in the Scope list.
Follow-up ticket 556a-5a1f-adb3-4139 is mentioned only for physical relocation of inbound_fields.py, not for documenting the protocol additions.
findings.finding: Two new Protocol method signatures (`FieldSanitizer.fit_comment` and `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text`) are added to `_backend.py` — the cross-backend contract that every future adapter implementor must satisfy — but the plan includes no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc addition documenting these new contract members and their semantics.
scenarios: A second backend implementor (the stated goal of epic bbf1) consults _backend.py and finds undocumented method contracts with no rationale for semantics (e.g., 'fit_comment vs sanitize_comment', 'identity for strings' contract on normalize_rich_text).
A reviewer auditing compliance of a new adapter against the port role has no ADR to reference for the 'fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation' vs 'sanitize' distinction.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py` docstring updates (or a short ADR) to the Scope list, specifying: (1) `fit_comment(body: str) -> str` — pure fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation, no send-side warning; (2) `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str` — ADF dict → text for Jira, identity for strings; Jira is the reference implementation. Cross-reference epic bbf1 and follow-up ticket 556a-5a1f.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `normalize_rich_text` on `InboundMapper` has ≥3 concrete call sites or justify the sub-threshold Protocol extension; also address `inbound_fields._normalize_jira_body` (inbound_differ.py:328) as a third near-identical private function that remains outside the port after this story.
evidence: Approach: 'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str (Jira: adf_to_text for dicts, identity for strings — exactly today's _normalize_adf_body, inbound_translate.py:349+)'
Remediation item 1: 'routes through the backend's InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text(body) (the SAME decode surface as the inbound apply path...)'
inbound_fields.py:131-138: _normalize_jira_body has identical semantics — already called at inbound_fields.py:159 and inbound_differ.py:328
Call-site count: outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body (dict branch, line 134) + inbound_translate._normalize_adf_body (re-impl) = 2 ports-level consumers
findings.finding: `normalize_rich_text` is proposed as a new `InboundMapper` Protocol method but the plan identifies only 2 call sites (inbound_translate._normalize_adf_body re-implementation + outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body ADF decode), which is below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a Protocol-level abstraction. Furthermore, `inbound_fields._normalize_jira_body` (inbound_fields.py:131, called at inbound_fields.py:159 and inbound_differ.py:328) is already a private implementation of the same semantics ('adf_to_text for dicts, str otherwise') — a third near-duplicate that the plan neither consolidates nor calls out.
findings.location: Approach section, 'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text'; Plan-review remediation, item 1
scenarios: A future backend implementor must add normalize_rich_text to their InboundMapper implementation for a method only called by 2 sites — the abstraction cost exceeds its coverage.
inbound_differ.py uses _normalize_jira_body directly from inbound_fields, bypassing the Protocol method; if the differ is ever ported it will need a third routing change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify `fit_comment` as a new `FieldSanitizer` Protocol method against Rule-of-Three (currently 1 call site at `outbound_comments.py:324`), or eliminate it by routing `:324` through the existing `sanitize_comment` (the warning is benign in a comparison-key context) or a parameter on `sanitize_comment`.
evidence: Remediation item 1 names a single call site: 'outbound_comments.py :324 compare-body truncation'
outbound_comments.py:324: compare_body = _load_comment_limits().truncate_comment_body(body) — the one site being replaced
jira_fields.py:184-210: _sanitize_comment already calls truncate_comment_body; the only difference is a logger.warning when truncation occurs
No other call sites for fit_comment named anywhere in plan or codebase
findings.finding: The remediation section introduces `FieldSanitizer.fit_comment` (a 'pure fit-to-limit, delegating to comment_limits.truncate_comment_body, no send-side warning') as a new Protocol method on `FieldSanitizer`, but the plan's only identified call site is `outbound_comments.py:324` (the compare-body truncation) — one call site, strictly below the Rule-of-Three threshold. The existing `sanitize_comment` already delegates to `truncate_comment_body`; the sole behavioural distinction is suppression of the warning log. A new Protocol method for a single call site whose only difference is logging level is premature abstraction.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation section, item 1: 'FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body) (a pure fit-to-limit, delegating to comment_limits.truncate_comment_body, no send-side warning) serves the :324 compare-body truncation'
scenarios: Every future backend must implement `fit_comment` in their FieldSanitizer for a method invoked from exactly one location.

A developer reading the Protocol sees two near-identical comment-truncation methods (sanitize_comment + fit_comment) differing only in warning behaviour, with no rule-of-three justification for the split.
findings.suggested_fix: Route outbound_comments.py:324 through `sanitizer.sanitize_comment(body)` instead of introducing `fit_comment`; the truncation warning on a comparison-key computation is informational-only and does not change correctness. If the warning is genuinely undesirable here, add a `warn: bool = True` kwarg to the existing `sanitize_comment` Protocol method rather than splitting into two methods.

findings.finding: AC3's proving method 'verified by reading the gate test' is not a machine-executable command — it relies on human visual inspection of the test file rather than a runnable assertion that fails automatically if the exemption, rationale, or ticket id are absent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'The sweep gate documents the single inbound_fields.py exemption with its rationale and the follow-up ticket id — verified by reading the gate test.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'verified by `grep -q "556a-5a1f-adb3-4139" tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py && grep -q "inbound_fields.py" tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py` passing in CI.'

findings.finding: `applier.py` imports `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, `_load_adf_module`, and `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate` at module scope (lines 111, 119, 124, 125) and re-exports all four in `__all__` (lines 322, 326, 361, 367). When the plan deletes `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`/`_AdfModule_Applier` and replaces `_normalize_adf_body` in `inbound_translate.py`, `applier.py`'s import block will raise `ImportError` at load time. `applier.py` is not in the plan's scope list.

`apply_inbound_records.py` imports `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate` (line 35) and calls it at three sites (lines 145, 268, 308). When `inbound_translate.py` removes or renames `_normalize_adf_body` as the plan requires, `apply_inbound_records.py` will fail with ImportError or NameError. It is not in the plan's scope list.

applier.py imports _load_adf_module from inbound_translate at module scope (line 124) and re-exports it in __all__ (line 361), but the plan deletes _ADF_KEY_APPLIER (inbound_translate:315) and its sole consumer _load_adf_module (lines 319-324) without listing applier.py in Scope — producing a broken module-level ImportError when applier is loaded after this change.
findings.suggested_fix: Add applier.py to the Scope file list. In the implementation, after routing _normalize_adf_body through the inbound mapper, either (a) delete _load_adf_module from inbound_translate.py AND remove its import/re-export from applier.py, or (b) keep _load_adf_module as a private helper in inbound_translate.py (it still loads the adf module, just not called by _normalize_adf_body) — but the simpler path is (a) since no external code calls applier._load_adf_module.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
